From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Stock character of a mysterious, beautiful, and seductive woman

For other uses, see [Femme Fatale (disambiguation)](/wiki/Femme_Fatale_(disambiguation) "Femme Fatale (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Spicy-Adventure_Stories_November_1936.png/250px-Spicy-Adventure_Stories_November_1936.png)](/wiki/File:Spicy-Adventure_Stories_November_1936.png)

*Femmes fatales* were standard fare in [hardboiled](/wiki/Hardboiled "Hardboiled") crime stories in 1930s [pulp fiction](/wiki/Pulp_magazine "Pulp magazine").

A ***femme fatale*** ([/ˌfɛm fəˈtæl, -ˈtɑːl/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*FEM fə-TA(H)L*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), French: [[fam fatal]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French"); lit. 'fatal woman'), sometimes called a **maneater**,[[1]](#cite_note-1) **Mata Hari**, or **vamp**, is a [stock character](/wiki/Stock_character "Stock character") of a mysterious, beautiful, and [seductive](/wiki/Seduction "Seduction") woman whose charms ensnare her lovers, often leading them into compromising, deadly traps. She is an [archetype](/wiki/Archetype "Archetype") of literature and art. Her ability to enchant, entice and hypnotize her victim with a spell was seen in the earliest stories as verging on [supernatural](/wiki/Supernatural "Supernatural"); hence, the *femme fatale* is often described as having a power akin to an enchantress, seductress, or [witch](/wiki/Witch "Witch"), having power over men. Femmes fatales are typically villainous, or at least morally ambiguous, and always associated with a sense of [mystification](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/mystification "wiktionary:mystification"), and unease.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

The term originates from the French phrase *femme fatale*, which means 'deadly woman' or 'lethal woman'. A *femme fatale* tries to achieve her hidden purpose by using feminine wiles such as beauty, charm, or sexual allure. In many cases, her attitude towards sexuality is [lackadaisical](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/lackadaisical "wiktionary:lackadaisical"), intriguing, or frivolous. In some cases, she uses lies or coercion rather than charm. She may also make use of some subduing weapon such as [sleeping gas](/wiki/Sleeping_gas "Sleeping gas"), a modern analog of magical powers in older tales. She may also be (or imply that she is) a victim, caught in a situation from which she cannot escape.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

In early 20th-century American films, a *femme fatale* character was referred to as a *vamp*, a reference to  *[The Vampire](/wiki/The_Vampire_(painting) "The Vampire (painting)")*, [Philip Burne-Jones](/wiki/Philip_Burne-Jones "Philip Burne-Jones")'s 1897 painting, and [Rudyard Kipling](/wiki/Rudyard_Kipling "Rudyard Kipling")'s later 1897 poem, and the 1909 play and 1915 film *[A Fool There Was](/wiki/A_Fool_There_Was_(1915_film) "A Fool There Was (1915 film)")*.

Female mobsters (including [Italian-American Mafia](/wiki/Italian-American_Mafia "Italian-American Mafia") or [Russian Mafia](/wiki/Russian_Mafia "Russian Mafia")) have been portrayed as femmes fatales in [films noir](/wiki/Films_noir "Films noir").[[4]](#cite_note-4) Femmes fatales are a recurring element in [James Bond](/wiki/James_Bond_(franchise) "James Bond (franchise)") films.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Femme_fatale&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Ancient archetypes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Femme_fatale&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Ancient archetypes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Wood_carving_detail2_-_Vishnu_Mohini.jpg/250px-Wood_carving_detail2_-_Vishnu_Mohini.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wood_carving_detail2_-_Vishnu_Mohini.jpg)

The divine *femme fatale* of Hindu lore, The goddess [Mohini](/wiki/Mohini "Mohini") is described to have enchanted gods, demons and sages alike.

The *femme fatale* [archetype](/wiki/Archetype "Archetype") exists in the culture, [folklore](/wiki/Folklore "Folklore") and myths of many cultures.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Ancient mythical or legendary examples include [Inanna](/wiki/Inanna "Inanna"), [Lilith](/wiki/Lilith "Lilith"), [Circe](/wiki/Circe "Circe"), [Medea](/wiki/Medea "Medea"), [Clytemnestra](/wiki/Clytemnestra "Clytemnestra"), [Lesbia](/wiki/Lesbia "Lesbia"), [Tamamo no Mae](/wiki/Tamamo_no_Mae "Tamamo no Mae"), and [Visha Kanyas](/wiki/Visha_Kanya "Visha Kanya"). Historical examples from classical times include [Cleopatra](/wiki/Cleopatra "Cleopatra") and [Messalina](/wiki/Messalina "Messalina"), as well as the biblical figures [Delilah](/wiki/Delilah "Delilah"), [Jezebel](/wiki/Jezebel "Jezebel"), and [Salome](/wiki/Salome "Salome").[[6]](#cite_note-6) An example from Chinese literature and traditional history is [Daji](/wiki/Daji "Daji").

### Early Western culture to the 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Femme_fatale&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early Western culture to the 19th century")]

The *femme fatale* was a common figure in the European [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), often portraying the dangers of unbridled female sexuality. The pre-medieval inherited biblical figure of [Eve](/wiki/Eve "Eve") offers an example, as does the wicked, seductive enchantress typified in [Morgan le Fay](/wiki/Morgan_le_Fay "Morgan le Fay"). The Queen of the Night in Mozart's *[The Magic Flute](/wiki/The_Magic_Flute "The Magic Flute")* shows her more muted presence during the [Age of Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The *femme fatale* flourished in the [Romantic](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism") period in the works of [John Keats](/wiki/John_Keats "John Keats"), notably "[La Belle Dame sans Merci](/wiki/La_Belle_Dame_sans_Merci "La Belle Dame sans Merci")" and "[Lamia](/wiki/Lamia_(poem) "Lamia (poem)")". Along with them, there rose the [gothic novel](/wiki/Gothic_novel "Gothic novel") *[The Monk](/wiki/The_Monk "The Monk")* by Matthew Gregory Lewis, featuring Matilda, a very powerful *femme fatale*. This led to her appearing in the work of [Edgar Allan Poe](/wiki/Edgar_Allan_Poe "Edgar Allan Poe"), and as the [vampire](/wiki/Vampire "Vampire"), notably in *[Carmilla](/wiki/Carmilla "Carmilla")* and *[Brides of Dracula](/wiki/Brides_of_Dracula "Brides of Dracula")*. *The Monk* was greatly admired by the [Marquis de Sade](/wiki/Marquis_de_Sade "Marquis de Sade"), for whom the *femme fatale* symbolised not evil, but all the best qualities of women; his novel *[Juliette](/wiki/L%27Histoire_de_Juliette "L'Histoire de Juliette")* is perhaps the earliest wherein the *femme fatale* triumphs. [Pre-Raphaelite](/wiki/Pre-Raphaelite "Pre-Raphaelite") painters frequently used the classic personifications of the *femme fatale* as a subject.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Franz_von_Stuck_010.jpg/250px-Franz_von_Stuck_010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Franz_von_Stuck_010.jpg)

[Salome](/wiki/Salome "Salome") in a 1906 painting by [Franz von Stuck](/wiki/Franz_von_Stuck "Franz von Stuck")

In the Western culture of the late 19th and early 20th centuries, the *femme fatale* became a more fashionable [trope](/wiki/Trope_(literature) "Trope (literature)"),[[8]](#cite_note-8) and she is found in the paintings of the artists [Edvard Munch](/wiki/Edvard_Munch "Edvard Munch"), [Gustav Klimt](/wiki/Gustav_Klimt "Gustav Klimt"), [Franz von Stuck](/wiki/Franz_von_Stuck "Franz von Stuck"), and [Gustave Moreau](/wiki/Gustave_Moreau "Gustave Moreau"). The novel *[À rebours](/wiki/%C3%80_rebours "À rebours")* by [Joris-Karl Huysmans](/wiki/Joris-Karl_Huysmans "Joris-Karl Huysmans") includes these fevered imaginings about an image of Salome in a Moreau painting:[[9]](#cite_note-9)

> No longer was she merely the dancing-girl who extorts a cry of lust and concupiscence from an old man by the lascivious contortions of her body; who breaks the will, masters the mind of a King by the spectacle of her quivering bosoms, heaving belly and tossing thighs; she was now revealed in a sense as the symbolic incarnation of world-old Vice, the goddess of immortal Hysteria, the Curse of Beauty supreme above all other beauties by the cataleptic spasm that stirs her flesh and steels her muscles, – a monstrous Beast of the Apocalypse, indifferent, irresponsible, insensible, poisoning.

— [Joris-Karl Huysmans](/wiki/Joris-Karl_Huysmans "Joris-Karl Huysmans"), *[À rebours](/wiki/%C3%80_rebours "À rebours")*, *Sisters of Salome*

In 1891, [Oscar Wilde](/wiki/Oscar_Wilde "Oscar Wilde") wrote the play *[Salome](/wiki/Salome_(play) "Salome (play)")*, in which Salome manipulates her lust-crazed stepfather, King Herod, with her enticing [Dance of the Seven Veils](/wiki/Dance_of_the_Seven_Veils "Dance of the Seven Veils") (Wilde's invention) to agree to her imperious demand: "bring me the head of [John the Baptist](/wiki/John_the_Baptist "John the Baptist")". Later, Salome was the subject of an opera by Strauss, and was popularized on stage, screen, and [peep show](/wiki/Peep_show "Peep show") booths in countless incarnations.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

She also is seen as a prominent figure in late 19th- and 20th-century opera, appearing in [Richard Wagner](/wiki/Richard_Wagner "Richard Wagner")'s *[Parsifal](/wiki/Parsifal "Parsifal")* ([Kundry](/wiki/Kundry "Kundry")), [Georges Bizet](/wiki/Georges_Bizet "Georges Bizet")'s *[Carmen](/wiki/Carmen "Carmen")*, [Camille Saint-Saëns](/wiki/Camille_Saint-Sa%C3%ABns "Camille Saint-Saëns")' *[Samson et Delilah](/wiki/Samson_et_Delilah "Samson et Delilah")* and [Alban Berg](/wiki/Alban_Berg "Alban Berg")'s *[Lulu](/wiki/Lulu_(opera) "Lulu (opera)")* (based on the plays *[Erdgeist](/wiki/Earth_Spirit_(play) "Earth Spirit (play)")* and *[Die Büchse der Pandora](/wiki/Pandora%27s_Box_(play) "Pandora's Box (play)")* by [Frank Wedekind](/wiki/Frank_Wedekind "Frank Wedekind")).

Other considerably famous *femmes fatales* include [Isabella of France](/wiki/Isabella_of_France "Isabella of France"), [Hedda Gabler](/wiki/Hedda_Gabler "Hedda Gabler") of Kristiania (now Oslo), [Marie Antoinette](/wiki/Marie_Antoinette "Marie Antoinette") of Austria, and, most famously, [Lucrezia Borgia](/wiki/Lucrezia_Borgia "Lucrezia Borgia").

#### Early 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Femme_fatale&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Early 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Thedarose.jpg/250px-Thedarose.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thedarose.jpg)

Actress Theda Bara, in the film *[A Fool There Was](/wiki/A_Fool_There_Was_(1915_film) "A Fool There Was (1915 film)")*

[Mrs Patrick Campbell](/wiki/Mrs_Patrick_Campbell "Mrs Patrick Campbell"), [George Bernard Shaw](/wiki/George_Bernard_Shaw "George Bernard Shaw")'s "second famed platonic love affair", (she published some of his letters)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) and [Philip Burne-Jones](/wiki/Philip_Burne-Jones "Philip Burne-Jones")'s lover and subject of his 1897 painting, *The Vampire*, inspired Burne-Jones's cousin [Rudyard Kipling](/wiki/Rudyard_Kipling "Rudyard Kipling") to write his poem "The Vampire", in the year [Dracula](/wiki/Dracula "Dracula") was published.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) The poem, which began: "A fool there was ...",[[17]](#cite_note-17) inspired [Porter Emerson Browne](/wiki/Porter_Emerson_Browne "Porter Emerson Browne") to write the play, *A Fool There Was*.

The poem was adapted to become a 1909 [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre") production. This was followed by The 1913 film *[The Vampire](/wiki/The_Vampire_(1913_film) "The Vampire (1913 film)")* by [Robert Vignola](/wiki/Robert_Vignola "Robert Vignola"), containing a "vamp" dance.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Protagonist [Alice Hollister](/wiki/Alice_Hollister "Alice Hollister") was publicised as "the original vampire".[[19]](#cite_note-Kalem-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) The 1915 film, [*A Fool There Was*](/wiki/A_Fool_There_Was_(1915_film) "A Fool There Was (1915 film)"), starring [Theda Bara](/wiki/Theda_Bara "Theda Bara"), as "The Vamp" followed.[[22]](#cite_note-SilentEra-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-:1-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) The short poem may have been used in the publicity for the 1915 film.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] 1910s [American](/wiki/American_English "American English") [slang](/wiki/Slang "Slang") for *femme fatale* was *vamp*, for *vampire*.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[24]](#cite_note-:1-24)

Another icon is Margaretha Geertruida Zelle. While working as an exotic dancer, she took the stage name [Mata Hari](/wiki/Mata_Hari "Mata Hari"). She was accused of German espionage during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") and was put to death by a French firing squad. After her death she became the subject of many sensational films and books.

Femmes fatales appear in [detective fiction](/wiki/Detective_fiction "Detective fiction"), especially in its 'hard-boiled' sub-genre which largely originated with the crime stories of [Dashiell Hammett](/wiki/Dashiell_Hammett "Dashiell Hammett") in the 1920s. At the end of that decade, the French-Canadian villainess [Marie de Sabrevois](/wiki/Marie_de_Sabrevois "Marie de Sabrevois") gave a contemporary edge to the otherwise historical novels of [Kenneth Roberts](/wiki/Kenneth_Roberts_(author) "Kenneth Roberts (author)") set during the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution").

A [jazz age](/wiki/Jazz_age "Jazz age") vamp (also known as a "baby vamp") was defined by [F. Scott Fitzgerald](/wiki/F._Scott_Fitzgerald "F. Scott Fitzgerald") in reference to one of his "careless" [flapper](/wiki/Flapper "Flapper") characters, as a [siren](/wiki/Siren_(mythology) "Siren (mythology)") - 'a picker up and thrower away of men, an unscrupulous and fundamentally unmoved toyer with affections.'[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Film villainesses often appeared foreign, often of Eastern European or Asian ancestry. They were a contrast to the wholesome personas of actresses such as [Lillian Gish](/wiki/Lillian_Gish "Lillian Gish") and [Mary Pickford](/wiki/Mary_Pickford "Mary Pickford"). Notable silent-cinema vamps include Theda Bara, [Helen Gardner](/wiki/Helen_Gardner_(actress) "Helen Gardner (actress)"), [Louise Glaum](/wiki/Louise_Glaum "Louise Glaum"), [Valeska Suratt](/wiki/Valeska_Suratt "Valeska Suratt"), [Musidora](/wiki/Musidora "Musidora"), [Virginia Pearson](/wiki/Virginia_Pearson "Virginia Pearson"), [Olga Petrova](/wiki/Olga_Petrova "Olga Petrova"), [Rosemary Theby](/wiki/Rosemary_Theby "Rosemary Theby"), [Nita Naldi](/wiki/Nita_Naldi "Nita Naldi"), [Pola Negri](/wiki/Pola_Negri "Pola Negri"), [Estelle Taylor](/wiki/Estelle_Taylor "Estelle Taylor"), [Jetta Goudal](/wiki/Jetta_Goudal "Jetta Goudal"), and, in early appearances, [Myrna Loy](/wiki/Myrna_Loy "Myrna Loy").

#### Classic film noir era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Femme_fatale&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Classic film noir era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Double-Indemnity-LIFE-1944-2.jpg/330px-Double-Indemnity-LIFE-1944-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Double-Indemnity-LIFE-1944-2.jpg)

*Femme fatale* [Phyllis Dietrichson](/wiki/Phyllis_Dietrichson "Phyllis Dietrichson"), played by [Barbara Stanwyck](/wiki/Barbara_Stanwyck "Barbara Stanwyck"), in the classic film noir *[Double Indemnity](/wiki/Double_Indemnity "Double Indemnity")*

During the era of classic [film-noirs](/wiki/Film-noir "Film-noir") of the 1940s and 1950s, the *femme fatale* flourished in American cinema. The archetypal *femme fatale* is [Phyllis Dietrichson](/wiki/Phyllis_Dietrichson "Phyllis Dietrichson"), played by [Barbara Stanwyck](/wiki/Barbara_Stanwyck "Barbara Stanwyck") (who was nominated for the [Academy Award for Best Actress](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Actress "Academy Award for Best Actress") for this role) in the 1944 film *[Double Indemnity](/wiki/Double_Indemnity "Double Indemnity")*. This character is considered one of the best *femme fatale* roles in film noir history.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The character was ranked as the #8 film villain of the first 100 years of American cinema by the [American Film Institute](/wiki/American_Film_Institute "American Film Institute") in the [AFI's 100 Years... 100 Heroes and Villains](/wiki/AFI%27s_100_Years..._100_Heroes_and_Villains "AFI's 100 Years... 100 Heroes and Villains").[[33]](#cite_note-AFI_List-33) In a classical film noir trope, she manipulates a man into killing her husband for financial gain.

Other examples of *femme fatale* include the [Dorothy Dandridge](/wiki/Dorothy_Dandridge "Dorothy Dandridge") as the titular character in *[Carmen Jones](/wiki/Carmen_Jones_(film) "Carmen Jones (film)")*, Brigid O'Shaughnessy, portrayed by [Mary Astor](/wiki/Mary_Astor "Mary Astor"), who murders Sam Spade's partner in *[The Maltese Falcon](/wiki/The_Maltese_Falcon_(1941_film) "The Maltese Falcon (1941 film)")* ([1941](/wiki/1941_in_film "1941 in film")); manipulative narcissistic daughter Veda (portrayed by [Ann Blyth](/wiki/Ann_Blyth "Ann Blyth")) in [*Mildred Pierce*](/wiki/Mildred_Pierce_(1945_film) "Mildred Pierce (1945 film)") who exploits her indulgent mother Mildred (portrayed by [Joan Crawford](/wiki/Joan_Crawford "Joan Crawford")) and fatally destroys her mother's remarriage to stepfather Monte Barragon (portrayed by [Zachary Scott](/wiki/Zachary_Scott "Zachary Scott")); [Gene Tierney](/wiki/Gene_Tierney "Gene Tierney") as Ellen Brent Harland in *[Leave Her to Heaven](/wiki/Leave_Her_to_Heaven "Leave Her to Heaven")* ([1945](/wiki/1945_in_film "1945 in film")), and the cabaret singer portrayed by [Rita Hayworth](/wiki/Rita_Hayworth "Rita Hayworth") in *[Gilda](/wiki/Gilda_(film) "Gilda (film)")* ([1946](/wiki/1946_in_film "1946 in film")),[[34]](#cite_note-Independent-34) narcissistic wives who manipulate their husbands; [Ava Gardner](/wiki/Ava_Gardner "Ava Gardner") in *[The Killers](/wiki/The_Killers_(1946_film) "The Killers (1946 film)")* and Cora ([Lana Turner](/wiki/Lana_Turner "Lana Turner")) in *[The Postman Always Rings Twice](/wiki/The_Postman_Always_Rings_Twice_(1946_film) "The Postman Always Rings Twice (1946 film)")*, based on novels by [Ernest Hemingway](/wiki/Ernest_Hemingway "Ernest Hemingway") and [James M. Cain](/wiki/James_M._Cain "James M. Cain") respectively, manipulate men into killing their husbands.[[34]](#cite_note-Independent-34)

In the [Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock") film *[The Paradine Case](/wiki/The_Paradine_Case "The Paradine Case")* ([1947](/wiki/1947_in_film "1947 in film")), [Alida Valli](/wiki/Alida_Valli "Alida Valli")'s character causes the deaths of two men and the near destruction of another. Another frequently cited example is the character Jane played by [Lizabeth Scott](/wiki/Lizabeth_Scott "Lizabeth Scott") in *[Too Late for Tears](/wiki/Too_Late_for_Tears "Too Late for Tears")* ([1949](/wiki/1949_in_film "1949 in film")); during her quest to keep some dirty money from its rightful recipient and her husband, she uses poison, lies, sexual teasing and a gun to keep men wrapped around her finger. [Jane Greer](/wiki/Jane_Greer "Jane Greer") remains notable as a murderous *femme fatale* using her wiles on [Robert Mitchum](/wiki/Robert_Mitchum "Robert Mitchum") in *[Out of the Past](/wiki/Out_of_the_Past "Out of the Past")* ([1947](/wiki/1947_in_film "1947 in film")). In *[Gun Crazy](/wiki/Gun_Crazy "Gun Crazy")* (1950), the *femme fatale* lures a man into a life of crime. In Hitchcock's [1940 film](/wiki/Rebecca_(1940_film) "Rebecca (1940 film)") and Daphne du Maurier's [1938 novel](/wiki/Rebecca_(novel) "Rebecca (novel)") *Rebecca*, the eponymous *femme fatale* completely dominates the plot, even though she is already dead and we never see an image of her. *[Rocky and Bullwinkle](/wiki/The_Adventures_of_Rocky_and_Bullwinkle_and_Friends_(TV_series) "The Adventures of Rocky and Bullwinkle and Friends (TV series)")*'s Natasha Fatale, a curvaceous spy, takes her name from the *femme fatale* stock character. *[Blonde Ice](/wiki/Blonde_Ice "Blonde Ice")* (1948) features a female serial killer who murders several men.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In *[Babylon](/wiki/Babylon_(2022_film) "Babylon (2022 film)")* (2022), [Margot Robbie](/wiki/Margot_Robbie "Margot Robbie") plays a character with *femme fatale* tendencies.[[36]](#cite_note-36) [Mia Goth](/wiki/Mia_Goth "Mia Goth") stars as a mysterious female character in [Infinity Pool](/wiki/Infinity_Pool_(film) "Infinity Pool (film)") (2023).

Academy Award-winning actress [Marion Cotillard](/wiki/Marion_Cotillard "Marion Cotillard") has frequently played femmes fatales, in such films as *[A Private Affair](/wiki/A_Private_Affair_(2002_film) "A Private Affair (2002 film)")* (2002), *[A Very Long Engagement](/wiki/A_Very_Long_Engagement "A Very Long Engagement")*, *[The Black Box](/wiki/La_Bo%C3%AEte_noire "La Boîte noire")*, *[Inception](/wiki/Inception "Inception")*, *[Midnight in Paris](/wiki/Midnight_in_Paris "Midnight in Paris")*, *[The Dark Knight Rises](/wiki/The_Dark_Knight_Rises "The Dark Knight Rises")* and *[Macbeth](/wiki/Macbeth_(2015_film) "Macbeth (2015 film)")*. In addition to *To Die For*, [Nicole Kidman](/wiki/Nicole_Kidman "Nicole Kidman") has played femmes fatales in films such as *[Malice](/wiki/Malice_(1993_film) "Malice (1993 film)")*, *[Moulin Rouge!](/wiki/Moulin_Rouge! "Moulin Rouge!")*, *[The Paperboy](/wiki/The_Paperboy_(2012_film) "The Paperboy (2012 film)")*, and *[The Northman](/wiki/The_Northman "The Northman")*.

The archetype is also abundantly found in American television. One of the most famous femmes fatales of American television is [Sherilyn Fenn](/wiki/Sherilyn_Fenn "Sherilyn Fenn")'s [Audrey Horne](/wiki/Audrey_Horne "Audrey Horne") of the [David Lynch](/wiki/David_Lynch "David Lynch") cult series *[Twin Peaks](/wiki/Twin_Peaks "Twin Peaks")*. In the TV series *[Femme Fatales](/wiki/Femme_Fatales_(TV_series) "Femme Fatales (TV series)")*, actress [Tanit Phoenix](/wiki/Tanit_Phoenix "Tanit Phoenix") played [Lilith](/wiki/Lilith "Lilith"), the host who introduced each episode [Rod Serling](/wiki/Rod_Serling "Rod Serling")-style and occasionally appeared within the narrative. In the [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") TV series *[Orange Is the New Black](/wiki/Orange_Is_the_New_Black "Orange Is the New Black")*, actress [Laura Prepon](/wiki/Laura_Prepon "Laura Prepon") played [Alex Vause](/wiki/Alex_Vause "Alex Vause"), a modern *femme fatale*, who led both men and women to their destruction.

Femmes fatales appear frequently in [comic books](/wiki/Comic_book "Comic book"). Notable examples include [Batman](/wiki/Batman "Batman")'s long-time nemesis [Catwoman](/wiki/Catwoman "Catwoman"), who first appeared in comics in 1940, and various adversaries of [The Spirit](/wiki/Spirit_(comics_character) "Spirit (comics character)"), such as [P'Gell](/wiki/P%27Gell "P'Gell").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

This stock character is also often found in the genres of opera and [musical theatre](/wiki/Musical_theatre "Musical theatre"), where she will traditionally have a [mezzo](/wiki/Mezzo-soprano "Mezzo-soprano"), [alto](/wiki/Alto "Alto") or [contralto](/wiki/Contralto "Contralto") range, opposed to the [ingénue](/wiki/Ing%C3%A9nue "Ingénue")'s [soprano](/wiki/Soprano "Soprano"), to symbolize the masculinity and lack of feminine purity.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] An example is Hélène from *[Natasha, Pierre and the Great Comet of 1812](/wiki/Natasha,_Pierre_and_the_Great_Comet_of_1812 "Natasha, Pierre and the Great Comet of 1812")*.

In more recent years, femmes fatales have been recognized in video games, an example of which being [Ada Wong](/wiki/Ada_Wong "Ada Wong") in the *[Resident Evil](/wiki/Resident_Evil "Resident Evil")* video game series.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

## Use in criminal trials

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Femme_fatale&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Use in criminal trials")]

The term has been used by the media in connection with highly publicised criminal trials, such as the trials of [Jodi Arias](/wiki/Murder_of_Travis_Alexander "Murder of Travis Alexander")[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39) and [Amanda Knox](/wiki/Amanda_Knox "Amanda Knox").[[40]](#cite_note-40)

## Further reading

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Femme_fatale&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Further reading")]

* ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Books-aj.svg_aj_ashton_01.png/40px-Books-aj.svg_aj_ashton_01.png)[Literature portal](/wiki/Portal:Literature "Portal:Literature")

* [Dominique Mainon](/wiki/Dominique_Mainon "Dominique Mainon") and [James Ursini](/wiki/James_Ursini "James Ursini") (2009) *Femme fatale*, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0879103698](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0879103698 "Special:BookSources/0879103698"). Examines the context of film noir.
* [Giuseppe Scaraffia](/wiki/Giuseppe_Scaraffia "Giuseppe Scaraffia") (2009) *Femme fatale*, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9788838903960](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9788838903960 "Special:BookSources/9788838903960")
* Julie Grossman (2020) *The Femme Fatale*, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9780813598246](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9780813598246 "Special:BookSources/9780813598246"). A brief history of the femme fatale in cinema and TV.
* Toni Bentley (2002) *Sisters of Salome*, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9780803262416](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9780803262416 "Special:BookSources/9780803262416"). Salome considered as an archetype of female desire and transgression and as the ultimate *femme fatale*.
* [Bram Dijkstra](/wiki/Bram_Dijkstra "Bram Dijkstra") (1986) *Idols of Perversity: Fantasies of Feminine Evil in Fin-De-Siecle Culture*, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0195056523](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0195056523 "Special:BookSources/0195056523"). Discusses the *Femme fatale*-stereotype.
* Bram Dijkstra (1996) *Evil Sisters: The Threat of Female Sexuality in Twentieth-Century Culture*, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0805055495](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0805055495 "Special:BookSources/0805055495").
* Elizabeth K. Mix *Evil By Design: The Creation and Marketing of the Femme Fatale*, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9780252073236](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9780252073236 "Special:BookSources/9780252073236"). Discusses the origin of the *Femme fatale* in 19th-century French popular culture.
* [Mario Praz](/wiki/Mario_Praz "Mario Praz") (1933) *The Romantic Agony*, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9780192810618](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9780192810618 "Special:BookSources/9780192810618"). See chapters four, 'La Belle Dame Sans Merci', and five, 'Byzantium'.
* Julie Grossman (2009) *Rethinking the Femme Fatale in film noir: Ready for her close-up*, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9781349313341](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9781349313341 "Special:BookSources/9781349313341"). Tries to bring about a more nuanced and sympathetic reading of the "femme fatale" in film criticism and popular culture commentary.
